title: Source-to-execution models

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Source-to-execution models; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Source-to-execution models
sources.claim: Source text reaches observable behavior through a named toolchain: a compiler may translate ahead of time, an interpreter may execute through another runtime, and a virtual machine may execute an intermediate form.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Source text reaches observable behavior through a named toolchain: a compiler may translate ahead of time, an interpreter may execute through another runtime, and a virtual machine may execute an intermediate form.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A C source file is passed to a compiler and linker, producing an executable that the operating system loads before its output appears. Your goal is to trace a high-level source file through a named toolchain to observable output. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Hardware and software roles. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Source text reaches observable behavior through a named toolchain: a compiler may translate ahead of time, an interpreter may execute through another runtime, and a virtual machine may execute an intermediate form. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Compiled and interpreted are not permanent language categories; one implementation can combine ahead-of-time compilation, bytecode, interpretation, and just-in-time compilation. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Compiled languages run directly from source while interpreted languages never translate any code.

includes: Recognize compilation, interpretation and virtual-machine execution without false absolutes.
Trace a high-level source file through a named toolchain to observable output.
excludes: Compiled and interpreted are not permanent language categories; one implementation can combine ahead-of-time compilation, bytecode, interpretation, and just-in-time compilation.

outcomes: Trace a high-level source file through a named toolchain to observable output.

examples.title: Source-to-execution models: direct case
examples.situation: A C source file is passed to a compiler and linker, producing an executable that the operating system loads before its output appears.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A C source file is passed to a compiler and linker, producing an executable that the operating system loads before its output appears.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Execution must name the concrete implementation and stages; source is represented or translated before a processor carries out machine instructions, and runtimes can mix strategies.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Execution must name the concrete implementation and stages; source is represented or translated before a processor carries out machine instructions, and runtimes can mix strategies.

examples.title: Source-to-execution models: boundary case
examples.situation: A Java source file becomes bytecode that a JVM loads and may interpret or compile further while the application is running.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A Java source file becomes bytecode that a JVM loads and may interpret or compile further while the application is running.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Compiled and interpreted are not permanent language categories; one implementation can combine ahead-of-time compilation, bytecode, interpretation, and just-in-time compilation.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Compiled languages run directly from source while interpreted languages never translate any code.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace a high-level source file through a named toolchain to observable output.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Compiled languages run directly from source while interpreted languages never translate any code.
misconceptions.correction: Execution must name the concrete implementation and stages; source is represented or translated before a processor carries out machine instructions, and runtimes can mix strategies.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Compiled languages run directly from source while interpreted languages never translate any code.

analogy.example: A workshop can model Source-to-execution models: instructions direct workers, the workbench holds active materials, and shelves preserve supplies between jobs.
usefulFor: Separating active execution, temporary state, persistent material, and coordinating roles
limitations: Computer components follow formal interfaces and timing rules that a human workshop does not reproduce.
Compiled and interpreted are not permanent language categories; one implementation can combine ahead-of-time compilation, bytecode, interpretation, and just-in-time compilation.

faded.prompt: Quick check for “Source-to-execution models: direct case”: state its starting evidence, apply the Source-to-execution models rule, and give the observable result in three short lines.
scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.
expectedEvidence: Named example: Source-to-execution models: direct case.
Starting evidence: A C source file is passed to a compiler and linker, producing an executable that the operating system loads before its output appears.

Core rule: Source text reaches observable behavior through a named toolchain: a compiler may translate ahead of time, an interpreter may execute through another runtime, and a virtual machine may execute an intermediate form.

Observable result: The case supports the declared outcome because Execution must name the concrete implementation and stages; source is represented or translated before a processor carries out machine instructions, and runtimes can mix strategies.
Boundary check: Execution must name the concrete implementation and stages; source is represented or translated before a processor carries out machine instructions, and runtimes can mix strategies.

recap.summary: You should now be able to trace a high-level source file through a named toolchain to observable output. Keep this boundary in view: Compiled and interpreted are not permanent language categories; one implementation can combine ahead-of-time compilation, bytecode, interpretation, and just-in-time compilation.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Source-to-execution models without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Compiled languages run directly from source while interpreted languages never translate any code.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A Java source file becomes bytecode that a JVM loads and may interpret or compile further while the application is running.
